title: SEGMENT 17 — Cardiac, lung, pancreas, prostate, breast: the organoid menagerie

lines.text: SEGMENT 17 — Cardiac, lung, pancreas, prostate, breast: the organoid menagerie

We've covered the major organoid systems in detail, but I want to briefly survey the rest of the menagerie, because the speed at which organoids of new tissues have been developed since 2009 is itself a striking feature of this field.
Within a few years of the original intestinal paper, recipes existed for organoids of essentially every major organ system.
Let me walk through the highlights.
Lung organoids.
Multiple groups, including Jason Spence's at Michigan, developed protocols for lung organoids in the mid-2010s.

These come in different flavors — proximal airway organoids (with cell types resembling trachea and bronchi), distal alveolar organoids (resembling the gas-exchange surface of the lung), and mixed organoids.
They've been used for respiratory infection studies (especially SARS-CoV-2 and influenza), cystic fibrosis modeling, lung cancer modeling, and drug screening for respiratory disease.
Pancreas organoids.
Both endocrine (insulin-producing) and exocrine (digestive-enzyme-producing) pancreatic organoids have been developed.
Pancreatic ductal organoids from the Clevers group are used heavily for pancreatic cancer modeling.

There's substantial interest in producing beta-cell-containing organoids that could one day be transplanted into diabetic patients to restore insulin production — Doug Melton's group at Harvard and others have made significant progress here, though the work is now more often done with directed differentiation of pluripotent cells into beta cells rather than as organoids per se.
Prostate organoids.
Important for prostate cancer modeling.
Patient-derived prostate cancer organoids have been used to test androgen-deprivation therapy responses and to study castration-resistant prostate cancer.
Breast organoids.
Mammary organoids, including patient-derived breast cancer organoids, are now used widely.
The Clevers group and others have built breast cancer biobanks.
There's also interest in normal mammary organoids for studying breast development and lactation biology.
Cardiac organoids.

These are trickier than epithelial organoids because the heart's architecture is not simply epithelial — it includes cardiomyocytes, fibroblasts, endothelial cells, and the geometric arrangements that allow coordinated contraction.
Several groups have developed cardiac organoids that beat spontaneously and show electrical properties resembling embryonic heart.
The work of Sasha Mendjan at IMBA in Vienna, among others, has produced cardiac organoids that recapitulate aspects of chamber formation.
These are being used for drug cardiotoxicity testing — a major cause of drug failure — and for modeling cardiac disease.
Vascular organoids.

Developed by Josef Penninger's group, these are made from pluripotent cells differentiated toward endothelial and mural cell lineages, producing networks of blood-vessel-like structures complete with capillaries and supporting cells.
These have been used to model diabetic vascular changes and SARS-CoV-2 vascular infection.
Stomach organoids.
Gastric organoids, including those modeling Helicobacter pylori infection and gastric cancer.
Salivary gland organoids.
Used to study salivary gland regeneration after radiation damage in head-and-neck cancer patients.
Thyroid organoids.
For studying thyroid development and disease.
Endometrial organoids.
Used to study fertility, menstrual biology, and endometrial cancer.
Choroid plexus organoids.
A relatively recent and interesting addition — the choroid plexus is the brain structure that produces cerebrospinal fluid.
These organoids can actually produce fluid that resembles CSF, and they've been used to study how things move from blood to brain.
The pattern across all these is consistent.
A few groups, often building on Clevers' original recipe with appropriate modifications, work out the right cocktail of growth factors for their tissue of interest.
The first paper shows that the organoids form.
Subsequent papers establish disease models, drug screens, biobanks.
Within a few years of the original publication, the technology is widely adopted and protocols are stable enough that any reasonably competent lab can produce them.
This is a remarkable acceleration of biological technology development.
From "we can't grow any organ in a dish" in 2008 to "we have published protocols for essentially every major organ" by 2020.
Twelve years.

That's how fast a single conceptual breakthrough — the Clevers/Sato insight that stem cells in the right environment will self-organize — can transform a discipline once the basic protocol exists and the community starts iterating.
Worth noting: not all organs are equally easy.
Some — intestine, liver, prostate — produce robust organoids with relatively simple protocols.
Others — heart, skin, complex brain regions — remain technically challenging and produce organoids that recapitulate only some features of the real organ.
The frontier of organoid development is, in part, about pushing the technique into the hardest tissues and getting them to behave.
But the menagerie is now broad enough that for most tissues a researcher might care about, an organoid protocol exists or can be adapted from a closely related tissue.
The field is at the point of maturation where new groundbreaking organoid systems are getting rarer, while refinement and application of existing ones is accelerating.
